scouting_report: Ariza is a budding jumbo wing who has glaring two-way tools and has continued to grow late into his high school years. Now standing at 6-foot-9 with a better than 7-foot wingspan, he has tremendous positional size and length. He's a fluid mover who runs the floor beautifully, covers the court, is a high-rising vertical athlete, and even has naturally soft hands and quick reactions. Ariza needs to keep getting stronger and fill out his frame, but can already fit into a high-level game. Defensively, those measurables and physical traits give him glaring upside and potential versatility. Offensively, he can be an explosive transition athlete, has a fluid release on his jumper out to the three-point line, and shows some flashes as a cutter, with his footwork, left-hand, and in the mid-post. He's also made significant strides as a passer, which is now potentially the most underrated part of his game. However, he isn't yet a natural creator or handler and isn't always a high-volume scorer either. So, while Ariza may struggle to score and pressure the rim at times, he still has a wealth of two-way tools that translate to the very highest levels with his size, length, athleticism, passing, shooting and defensive potential.

Kinney is a scoring and playmaking lead guard who is smooth and versatile with his attack. He’s a multi-level scoring threat with a creative and crisp handle to get to his advanced pull-up game or pressure the rim. He can rise-up to finish when he has momentum, and also owns dexterity and touch as a lay-up maker. He’s a bit streakier from long-range, with a tendency to short-arm that release more, (33% in OTE & 27% in 3SSB), but plenty capable when in a good rhythm. While Kinney has trended towards more of a scoring lead guard recently, he also has good natural instincts as a passer, averaging 5 assists per game in both OTE & 3SSB. He’s great in drive-and-kick actions, can make pick-and-roll reads, and understands how to feed the post. He also has a pretty good feel, even while in attack mode, of how to utilize his versatility to make plays without pounding the ball or settling unnecessarily. Where he’s a little less natural at this point, is in the rare situation when he doesn’t have the ball in his hands. Defensively, he’s good with his hands (1.3 steals in 3SSB play) and shown flashes of being able to blow-up ball screens when he’s fully engaged on that end, which isn’t totally consistent yet. He’s also a good rebounding guard (5.8 per game). Physically, while he measures in at just over 6-foot-1, he plays much bigger than his size, thanks his long 6-foot-6-plus wingspan, increased muscle mass, and the physicality in his game.

Adkins is one of the most naturally talented frontcourt prospects in the class of 2026 and while his tools have been glaring for some time now, he's just starting to turn potential into production at a more consistent rate. A long and athletic southpaw, Adkins now measures at 6-foot-9 with shoes on, has a massive 7-foot-2 wingspan, nearly 9-foot standing reach, and huge hands. He runs the floor well, is a bouncy vertical athlete, and major lob threat who can rise-up for emphatic finishes. He’s also a major shot-blocking presence (2.3 per game in 3SSB play) with a wealth of defensive upside. The single biggest variable in Adkins’ game is his motor. When he’s fully engaged and committed to being a rim runner, shot-blocker, and lob threat, he can control the paint on both ends of the floor. When he doesn’t play with the necessary energy, that’s when his production falls off. Once his motor becomes a constant, there are secondary areas of improvement that could take his game to new levels as well. He’s much more of a play finisher, than playmaker. He's just starting to develop some footwork around the paint and a jump hook, but needs to continue getting stronger, raise his release point in the lane, improve his right hand, and ability to put the ball on the floor when facing up. He also has soft natural touch (73% FT) and may be able to space the floor both vertically and with his shooting eventually, but isn’t yet consistent from long range (1-16 3pt). He’s a more active rebounder on the offensive end (just 3.8 of his 6.6 rebounds were on the defensive glass) and has yet to fully embrace his significant defensive upside.

Moody is a two-way combo-guard who had a big early reputation, but suffered a significant setback with a torn ACL as an underclassmen. He's worked his way back in the years since, and is now starting to realize some of that early potential. He has a wiry build, good open floor speed, and the athleticism to impact the game both attacking the rim and defensively. He's best when not asked to be a full-time point guard, but he has the versatility to play on or off the ball, the handle to make plays in space, and the burst to infuse tempo into the game. The most notable area of improvement as of late has been with his shooting. Historically unreliable from three, his release has gotten more fluid and consistent as he prepares for Darien DeVries system. His decision making has also come a long way as he's had stretches in the past of forcing the action or settling for tough-shots, but has recently found a nice balance of playing within the flow.

Stocky move tight end who can fluidly run a full route tree and separate at all three levels of the defense. Primarily works as a slot receiver at the prep level, and helped lead Lexington to the region 10 semifinals as a junior. Hovers around 6-foot-3, 230 pounds and excels at finding soft spots in coverage before making defenders miss in the open field. Not a straight-line burner, but has plenty of lower body explosiveness which is backed up by above-the-rim basketball experience. Earned Division I hoops offers from Arizona State and Texas A&M as a sophomore. Limited blocking reps at this stage, but has proven effective stalk-blocking and walling off defenders from the slot. Profiles as an unattached receiving tight end who can move around the formation to hunt mismatches in 12 personnel sets. Should be viewed as a developmental tight end prospect with NFL Draft upside, who could add layers at the first two levels for a CFP contender's passing attack.

Philon is a wiry built, run-and-jump athlete who can impact the game on both ends of the floor. At 6-foot-8 with a 6-foot-11 wingspan, he’s still filling out a relatively lean frame, but has speed and explosiveness when he turns the jets on. He’s an elite defensive prospect and has playmaking instincts as well as athleticism on the offensive end, but is not yet much of a shooting threat with a visible hitch in his overhead release (23% 3pt and 58% FT in UAA play). Defensively, he changes the game with his switch-ability, weak-side shot-blocking (1.6 per game), and instincts in passing lanes (1.2 steals). He's the type of defender who can both guard the other team's best player, but also erase mistakes for his team as a roaming playmaker off the ball. He’s at his best when able to turn defense into offense, because he’s not just a dynamic above-the-rim finisher, but can also make plays with the ball in open space. He shows flashes of passing, but can try to do too much at times (1.8 assists vs. 2.5 turnovers), and also needs to build-up his body to absorb contact in a half-court game. He’s a good offensive rebounder, and constant tip-dunk threat, but less active on the defensive glass. Philon can be so impactful defensively that he's one of the few players in high school who can completely change a game without needing the ball in his hands. As he continues to get stronger and improve his shooting, there's a long runway for him to continue to evolve and allow the rest of his game to catch-up.

County is strong and physical with long arms and loads of winning intangibles. While he’s a versatile scoring threat and capable of creating his own shot from all three levels, he understands how to play within the flow, move the ball, and keep the dominos falling when the opposing defense is scrambling (3.4 assists with 2.1 turnovers). That requires both unselfishness and a high basketball IQ. Similarly, he throws ahead in transition, makes quick/simple reads with the ball, cuts well, and just knows how to play in offensive structure. He’s shown significant growth with his handle and is blossoming into a legit big guard because of it. He’s always had a good left hand, as both a driver and a finisher, and been able to absorb contact. Now we’re seeing a little bit of twitch, an understanding of how to use his big frame to play out of pick-and-roll, and enough reliability with the ball to be a secondary handler. He’s a good pull-up shooter ad also a threat from three, but will need to smooth out his release from long-range (30% 3pt with a notable wider base), particularly when he’s rushed or on the move. Overall, Country is trending towards someone who may be able to play, and defend, three different positions. He’s physical and competitive on the defensive end, he communicates, and is also a good rebounding guard (5.1). The bottom line is that his size, length, strength, two-way versatility, high basketball IQ, and competitive intangibles all him to impact winning in a wide variety of ways.

A former football quarterback turned point guard, Ertel is as good as it gets in terms of toughness and competitive intangibles. But beyond his motor, the southpaw is also skilled and smart with evolving playmaking ability. In short, he may not have glaring physical upside, and is older for his grade, but has all the makings of a classic Purdue overachiever. He made 37% of his threes in 3SSB play, but a whopping 53% on unguarded catch-and-shoot attempts, with a quick release when in rhythm. His head is constantly up off the dribble and he’s a good passer with both hands (5.9 assists to 2.4 turnovers). He’s strong with the ball, changes speeds to get into the lane, and has a floater game. While he lacks elite length or athleticism, and shot just below 50% at the rim, there’s nonetheless growing pop and physicality in the lane. He's going to be at his best defensively in gaps, and when not overextended, but his intelligence, toughness, and quick hands (1.9 steals) are all major assets on that end. He’s also a terrific rebounding guard, who even sneaks into the offensive glass at times.

Bivins is undersized and not a dynamic athlete, but his overlap of skill and feel for the game is among the very best in the class. He can dance with his handle and string together rhythmic combo-moves as if he were in an individual workout. He only needs the slightest bit of separation in order to splash tough shots with deep range. Inside the lane, he’s extremely crafty with a great left hand, advanced floater game, and a ton of tricks. He’s also a very talented passer who led 3SSB in assists for most of the season, before finishing at just under 5 per game. Bivins shot just 25% from behind the arc in the grassroots season, but has the touch (90% FT) and mechanics to be much better than that with improved shot-selection at the next level. Because he can’t always separate with his first-step or explode in the lane, there are times he has to rely on plays with high degrees of difficultly, which can impact his efficiency (37% FG), but that too could evolve when he has more talent around him. There are going to be times where his size limits him defensively as well, but he nonetheless has terrific hands. They’re quick, twitchy, and able to take virtually anything they touch (2.3 steals).

Rice is a wing shooter with good positional size and feel for the game. His mechanics are very fluid with a quick and compact release. He has clear gravity as a floor-spacer, but is particularly dangerous shooting off the dribble, both from long-distance as well as with a complimenting mid-range pull-up game. He knows how to play in offensive structure, moves without the ball intelligently, and is a willing ball-mover. He’s not much of a creator yet though and needs to keep developing his handle, particularly going left. Physically, Rice lacks ideal length, has a high center of gravity, and currently lean build which allows him to get bumped around at times. He’s a solid leaper when he has space to elevate off two feet and a good wing rebounder (7.4 per game in UA Rise). Defensively, he moves pretty well laterally and had solid playmaking metrics (1.4 blocks and 1.3 steals), but might be a bit stiff in his hips and sometimes guilty of playing to the level of his competition, showing more fire in settings like the NBPA Top 100 Camp then we always did in the grassroots season.

Portela is versatile and efficient wing trending towards a big guard. He’s effective in an up-tempo style because he fills lanes and finishes with real speed in transition, but also really knows how to play in the half-court. He's quick to the cup as a slasher and handles it well enough to be a secondary ball-handler, which just makes his perimeter size that much more valuable. He's a solid passer, reliable with the ball, and able to play in the flow of the game without needing to be an offensive focal point (56% FG and 2 assists vs. 1.7 turnovers). His shooting, which is a key long-term swing skill, has improved. He still has a little hitch in his release, but is very selective with his looks, which allowed him to knock down 39% of his threes during 3SSB play on just over 1 attempt per game. He's also a consistent free-throw shooter, who has composure down the stretch of close games. While he's not always a high-volume scorer, Portela is a productive and competitive two-way player. He's very engaged defensively and capable of locking up opposing scorers. While his frame is still lean, he's stronger than he looks, and rarely gets leveraged into bad spots. He averaged 1.7 steals and 0.7 blocks per game in 3SSB play and should be increasingly capable of guarding multiple positions as he builds up his body. Portela is a bit older for his grade and turned 19 in December of his senior year.

Allmond is a former undersized big man who is now trending towards a versatile, inside-out four. He has a naturally broader frame with long arms, good hands, touch, and a physical style. While he’s now capable of scoring from different spots on the floor, he’s still best inside the lane. He has a legitimate post-game with terrific footwork, both before and after the catch. He knows how to get open on the block, seal for high-low angles, and can create his own shot when his defender plays behind him. He’s also a solid athlete at the rim and high-volume rebounder (8.1 per game in 3SSB play), particularly on the offensive end (2.8). His face-up game has made notable strides. He’s very comfortable at the high post, has a soft mid-range touch, can put the ball on the floor a couple of times to either side, and make rhythm step-in threes (36% in 3SSB play on nearly 3 attempts per game). There’s still room for growth with his handle, and even his finishing (54% on non-dunks), as he tends to give away some of his length with a lower release point. He shows flashes of passing potential, but can be turnover prone when being overzealous looking to make plays (1.4 assists from 2.1 turnovers). Allmond was an early-bloomer, who wasn’t juts bigger and stronger than his peers at an early stage, but also older. He turns 19 in October of his senior year. So while he has less glaring untapped physical upside, the continued growth of his game will come in correlation to his expanding versatility. Defensively, he plays with good energy and has sneaky versatility as well. He gets into a good low stance, works to slide his feet on the perimeter, and has no problem bodying up with bigger guys inside. He also provides some real rim protection (1.9 bloks per game) and generally active hands (0.8 steals).

Chronister is a shooting wing with good positional size. He’s a bit of a specialist who took nearly two-thirds of his shots from behind the arc in the recent Pro16 season, and over 80% in EYBL Scholastic play as a junior. While he doesn’t get much lift, hopping into a wide base, he has a high and compact release that he can get off without much separation. He’s equally dangerous shooting off the catch or the dribble and possesses that clear confidence and swagger that so many shot-makers share. He’s reliable with the ball, can make some heady passes when his feet are set in the half-court, and even occasionally lead the break. While Chronister isn’t bad with his handle, he lacks the first-step or vertical explosiveness to be a creator. He provides gravity as a floor-spacer and attack close-outs utilizing a little shot-fake, but is still working on finishing through contact in the lane. Defensively, he compensates for his lack of footspeed with clear competitiveness, but is still sometimes better suited defending fours than threes, and has had the benefit of playing alongside highly switchable frontcourt defenders at various stops.

Brown is coming off a breakout EYBL campaign in which he averaged 18.9 points. 4.7 assists, and 3.5 rebounds while leading a Team Durant squad that made the elite eight at the Peach Jam. He’s a scoring and playmaking lead guard with skill, strength, physicality, and vision. He can get buckets in bunches when he gets going and make some high-level passes. He’ll snap balls off the dribble, make good reads off ball-screens, and is an impressive lob thrower. He’s a scoring threat at all three levels who has deep shooting range when his feet are under him, can make pull-ups, and tough lay-ups at the rim with both hands. He does have a high-volume style though and is too willing to settle for tough shots, which can impact his overall efficiency numbers (40% FG, 30% 3pt, & 3.4 turnovers per game). He also lacks ideal size, length, or explosiveness for the very highest levels and can get swallowed up if a crowded lane collapses on him, so he tends to be most effective when playing north to south with the ball, where he can push to create pace and space to operate in. Defensively, he’s physical and competitive, but best when not over-exposed too far away from his help and also not especially switchable because of his lack of size. The biggest long-term question though is how he adapts when he doesn’t have the same extreme levels of volume and freedom that he’s enjoyed during both the high school and EYBL seasons.

Oladotun is a late-blooming jumbo wing, who has continued to grow throughout his high school years and now stands at 6-foot-10 with a 7-foot-1 wingspan. He’s still physically undeveloped with a lean frame, boxy shoulders, and high center of gravity, but isn’t apprehensive to play through contact and only going to keep getting better as he grows into his body. His game, like his frame, is still a work-in-progress, but loaded with long-term potential. It’s the vast scope of his versatile tools that is perhaps most compelling. He’s already a threat to make plays as a handler, shoot over top of contesting defenders, or take smaller defenders into the mid-post. He tends to be a bit upright, but he has good hands and is very comfortable with his dribble. He made just 28% of his threes and 63% of his free-throws in EYBL play, and has an elbow that can fly out on his way up into his release, but still soft natural shooting touch to develop. Where he really excels is with his footwork. It comes out in the mid-post area, but even more so at the end of his drives. He has an instinctive ability to navigate the lane in ways that can’t be taught, with a full repertoire of maneuvers after he picks up his dribble. He decelerates well, utilizes euros, but also knows when to elongate his strides. He’ll even mix in pro-hops, spins, fake-spins, and step-throughs. He’s a solid athlete, who can jump off either one foot or two, and can cover ground as more of a long-jumper, but it’s really the footwork between his last dribble and before his finish that is so elite. Defensively, his size, fluidity, and active hands are all assets, but his high hips aren’t ideal, particularly for someone who plays fairly upright and without a great amount of bend.

Bouie is undeniably gifted and a bit of a late-bloomer. While he averaged 15 points and nearly 6 rebounds per game this year in the EYBL, his best basketball is still in front of him with some glaring tools. He measured at 6-foot-4 in June of his sophomore year, but had sprouted up almost two inches in the next 12 months and now has a 6-foot-10 wingspan to match. There are some dynamic flashes in the open floor as he can cover the court with long strides and then rise-up quickly for explosive finishes. When he gets rolling, he can also string together some intriguing moments in the half-court as well with glimpses of shot-making, a quick first-step, and even an occasional cut off the ball. The biggest problem right now is consistency and a lot of that comes down to strength, physicality, and decision-making. His lanky frame needs a significant amount of muscle mass to play through contact. He gets bumped off the line of his drives and displaced defensively as well. While he has a good left hand, he tries to do too much with the ball at times, and needs to simplify his attack in order to be more reliable (1.9 assists vs. 3.2 turnovers per game). Similarly, while there is long-term shooting potential, he converted just 23% his attempts from behind the arc and is probably even farther away in terms of shooting off the dribble. The bottom line is that Bouie has a very high upside, has made some clear strides within the last year, and still has a lot of room for future growth that could potentially take his game to new levels.

Adonis has a similarly rare combination of physical gifts. At 6-foot-11, he’s a fluid mover, bouncy athlete, and the more skilled of the two twins. His soft touch already extends out to the arc, where he’s a legitimate shooting threat. He’s even shown flashes of being able to elevate into pull-ups off the dribble or from the mid-post, but has a relatively low release point that gives away some of his length. While Adonis is the more skilled of the two brothers, he’s also the less physical. They both need to get stronger, but he doesn’t have the same appetite for physicality that his brother does. As a result, he doesn’t impact the game as much as he could inside the paint, on either end of the floor. Despite that, and a similar lack of length for his size, he still has all the natural tools needed to be an extremely versatile two-way threat. The durability to keep acquiring game reps will help him to make quicker reads and more instinctive plays on both ends. So, while he too has to keep getting more consistent before his production is as reliable as his potential, his long-term ceiling is very high. There just aren’t that many near seven-footers out there who are graceful movers, bouncy leapers, and capable of making threes in bunches when they get hot.

Scott is a high upside big wing who is early in his overall development, but has significant long-term potential. He's measured at over 6-foot-7 without shoes and has long arms with a 7-foot-1 wingspan. Beyond that, he's a fluid mover and bouncy vertical athlete. Those physical tools give him obvious defensive upside, not just on the ball but on the weakside as well. Offensively, there are flashes of developing ball skills with shooting, handling, an occasional pass, and even an instinct for moving without the ball, but none of it is quite yet as polished as it needs to be. So, Scott is going to require some patience. He needs to build-up his body, learn to play through contact, and just gain more game experience against quality competition. What's undeniable though is that he has extreme tools and a vast amount of potential to develop, on both ends of the floor, in the coming years.

Smrekar is a big perimeter player with great natural feel for the game, basketball IQ, and passing ability. At 6-foot-7, he’s built like a wing, but can initiate offense like a lead guard. He sees the floor, can make reads out of ball-screens, and is the type of quick decision-maker who frequently sees where the ball needs to go before he catches it. He is an instinctive cutter who moves well without the ball and thrives in offensive structure. Smrekar can also take smaller defenders into the post, utilize his size to score over top of them, or facilitate for others off the block. In addition to his excellent perimeter size, Smrekar has a strong frame that should continue to fill-out. He’s not lightning quick, but has a bit of deceptive vertical athleticism at the rim. While he’s going to be capable of playing multiple positions, and spots on the floor, offensively, he’s going to be at his best defensively against opposing wings where he isn’t giving up too much footspeed. He also needs to get more consistent with his outside shooting after converting 26% of his three-point attempts in the 2025 FIBA U19 World Cup and 30% in the 2024 FIBA U18 European Championships.

Morano Mahmutovic is a wing shooter with good positional size. He has undeniable gravity spacing the floor, but can also make different types of shots, on the move or off the dribble, thus making him the type of threat that Gerry McNamara will be able to run pays for. Physically, he has some athletic limitations, could better sculpt his frame, and tends to play with an upright posture. That will correlate to some challenges on the defensive end of the floor. Offensively, he has some handle and can be crafty in the lane, but will have to adjust to the speed and physicality of the American game.

Martinsen is a smart and skilled perimeter forward. The southpaw is best utilized as a facilitating wing offensively, but frequently best defending opposing fours. He has excellent perimeter footwork, a functional handle, and can utilize both to create space for his pull-up game. He’s very comfortable in the mid-range area, and can even make tough step-backs off his left foot, but doesn’t get all the way to the rim quite as consistently. He’s a consistent shooting threat out to the arc with soft natural touch, but not yet knockdown enough to be a specialist (34% on nearly 5 attempts per game in UAA play and 61% free-throw). Martinsen is very heady. He sees the floor well, hits cutters, can pass off the dribble, and can facilitate from different spots on the floor. While he’s capable of playing multiple positions, he creates the most mismatches, and has the best defensive match-ups, at the four. He doesn’t have totally ideal size for that spot, but can compensate with above-average length, good shoulders, and a solid build. He’s not a bad vertical athlete in space, and gradually becoming more willing to play through contact, but needs to be a more consistently diligent rebounder (2.6 per game).

McCoy has evolved into one of the most physically imposing guards in the class. He has good size at just under 6-foot-5, a now powerful build after putting on notable muscle mass within the last year, long arms with an almost 6-foot-10 wingspan, and a combination of speed and power with the basketball that allows him to attack the rim violently in the open floor. McCoy is also one of the very best defenders in the class. His size, length, and strength are all assets but most of all he’s competitive with a high-motor. He can guard multiple positions and simultaneously pressure the ball, but is also a playmaker off the ball. He communicates well, directs traffic, and can cover up mistakes when he’s allowed to roam from the weakside (1.6 steals & 1.2 blocks per game in the EYBL). Finally, McCoy is an outstanding rebounding guard who loves to take the ball off the defensive glass and look to get downhill right away (6.8 per game). Offensively, he plays with a lot of sheer force, whether that be by getting downhill, attacking the offensive glass (2.8 per game), or finishing at the rim (98th percentile). He doesn’t necessarily separate quite as easily in the half-court though, and while he can be jittery with his handle and use his physicality to draw fouls, his total ball skills could tighten. He’s trending as more of an off-ball guard right now, and while he can make some tough pull-ups, his ability to space the floor off the catch is a significant swing skill (30% 3pt & 56% FT in EYBL). Overall, he is a physically gifted guard who plays with a motor, imposes his will on the game on both ends of the floor, and has room for future growth with the progression of his shooting and total guard skills.

Williams is a rapidly ascending prospect with a wealth of long-term tools, and while he is not yet a finished product by any means, he has one of the higher long-term upsides in the class. Now standing at 6-foot-11 with shoes on, he has mobility, athleticism, budding face-up skill, and defensive versatility alike. He’s a bouncy leaper, excellent runner, and can really cover the court at his size. He has soft natural touch and shows shooting potential to not only space the floor, but even shoot off the dribble, even with a relatively quick natural release. He’s comfortable putting the ball on the floor a bit, albeit a bit upright, and has a terrific left hand, even making tough finishes around the rim. Defensively, he’s a true rim protector (3.3 per game in 3SSB play) and active rebounder (8.5 per game, including 2.4 offensive). He’s diligent about getting into a stance when pulled to the perimeter, proactive moving his feet, and disciplined contesting shots, even if he’s not yet great laterally or always polished with the angle of his feet. Physically, he’s cut up, but still lean with a more naturally narrow core (7-foot-1.5 wingspan). He’s not averse to playing through contact, and has good natural body control even when knocked off his spots, but his game should go to new levels as he adds more muscle mass.

Obenyah could be a jumbo lead guard in the making. He has terrific positional size, broad shoulders, and good length. He played with the ball in his hands this season and while he was turnover prone at times, he showed a good feel for the game and passing ability with both hands (4.2 assists to 3.1 turnovers). He’s not necessarily a twitchy athlete, but he plays through contact and has plenty of room on his frame to keep filling out. Defensively, he can move his feet, guard the ball, and should be versatile with his size, strength, and movement skills. He’s also a good perimeter rebounder (4.9) on both ends of the floor. The major limitation in his game right now is that he’s not a reliable perimeter shooter (22% from three with suspect set-shot mechanics).

Samuels is a volume scoring lefty wing. He may hunt his offense, and try to score on virtually every catch, but there are few players in the country who can put points on the board in bunches like he can when he gets hot. While he would undoubtedly benefit from being a more willing ball-mover (1.4 assists to 1.7 turnovers) and picking his spots a little more selectively, he has the high release point to make tough shots without much separation, both in the mid-range area and behind the arc. He’s lethal with his pull-up game, shooting 38% from behind the arc off the dribble, and has a complimenting floater game as well. Maybe the most undervalued part of his game is his ability to come off ball-screens as he ranked in the 98th percentile in EYBL play with 1.23 points per possession. He likes to use his handle to create space, but is at his best when also looking to put pressure on the rim. He can get downhill in the open floor and will go right at opposing shot-blockers and often try to challenge them body-to-body. While that approach can draw contact and get him to the free-throw line, there are also times he could use more finishing craft. Physically, he is naturally strong with long arms (6-foot-11 wingspan). He’s made solid strides athletically, but is not a naturally dynamic mover. He shows flashes of being a high-volume wing rebounder and active defender, but too often his intensity and attention to detail on that end of the floor comes in correlation to his offensive output. In short, when he’s rolling, he’s one of the best players in the country, putting up big numbers, scoring at all 3 levels, defending, and rebounding. Consistency is the key though. There are still times, when he can be ball dominant, settle for tough shots at two levels, and allow his overall motor to be predicated on whether or not the ball is going in.

What makes Egbuonu almost unique is his overlap of physical strength, length, and basketball skill. He's built like a football player with a super powerful upper body, long arms, and good hands. He's capable of going right through contact off the dribble, can navigate the lane with good footwork, but has the high release point to shoot over top of contesting defenders. His range also extends out to the three-point line, making him a three-range threat. The consistency of his shooting is a major swing-skill and key to opening up a lot of his playmaking opportunities. He shot 27% from behind the arc in 3SSB play and looks more comfortable off a rhythm dribble. He’s a solid vertical athlete, with a second bounce, but not as quick with his first-step or laterally. He’s sometimes best defending opposing fours, but has no problem matching up with bigger guys, and is also a good rebounder (6.4). Overall, Egbuonu is a combo-forward capable of creating mismatches with his length, strength, and versatile skill, but needs to keep working on his foot speed and the consistency of his shooting.

Thomas is a skilled and smooth guard. He’s had clear ability with the ball since he was younger and those tools have only grown with time. He’s shifty with his handle, able to attack both sides, and a good passer, who keeps his head up and maintains vision even while pushing the tempo. He has a soft natural shooting touch and is a threat to make both pull-ups and threes. His release tends to come out a little low, and sometimes towards the left side of his head, but it’s quick with good rotation. He likes to shoot off the dribble, but is more efficient shooting off the dribble. Thomas has built up his body, gotten stronger, and gradually added more burst off the bounce, but still doesn’t have elite athletic pop or explosiveness. Defensively, he gets into a good low stance, but isn’t necessarily quick out of it. The biggest key for him if handling adversity and not letting mistakes multiply. He can be vulnerable to those stretches at times and that’s the root of his high turnover rate (4.3 assists vs. 3.6 turnovers), when he’s otherwise reliable with the ball.

Mayfield has glaring physical tools and is just scratching the surface of his potential. He has good size at 6-foot-7-plus, extreme length with a 7-foot-1 wingspan, and a naturally strong frame that continues to fill-out. He’s an explosive vertical athlete who gets up with force to throw down some violent finishes at the rim. Beyond his physical tools, Mayfield has the foundation of some inside-out versatility. He’s got some shooting potential out to the arc, a little bit of early handle when he’s in space, and spurts of footwork and physicality at the rim. Defensively, he provides some real rim protection from the weak-side of the floor (1.7 blocks and 0.9 steals in UAA play). Mayfield is still in the early stages of putting all his tools together though. He’s younger for his grade and won’t turn 18 until March of his senior year. He’s also relatively unchallenged during the high school season, and able to thrive on physical tools alone, so he’ll have to adjust to quality levels of competition. There are times when he can drift and not make his presence felt. For all his versatile potential, nothing is quite college ready just yet, and his decision-making as a passer is a clear work in progress (0.9 assists vs. 2.7 turnovers). Long-term, he could very well emerge into a big wing, but he plays, and moves, like more of an inside-out hybrid-four right now.

Keita is a long and athletic developing big man who measured at 6-foot-10-and-three-quarters at last year's NBPA Top 100 Camp along with a 7-foot-7 wingspan and 9-foot-4-and-a-half standing reach. He's also a big-time mover for his size who has quick feet and agility, in addition to the ability to beat opposing bigs up and down the floor. Defensively, he's a rim protector but also has some ability away from the basket. He has the potential to defend ball-screens in multiple ways and maybe even be somewhat switchable. He's proactive jabbing at the ball, active with his hands, has the tools to recover, and erase mistakes. Offensively, his quickness allows him to separate quickly as a roller, he's a bouncy finisher at the rim, and is a lob threat. He also shows some touch and passing instincts to develop in time. Keita just turned 18 years old and still has a largely undeveloped frame. He's going to need to get a lot stronger and the physicality of the college game may be his most significant immediate challenge, especially against high-level competition that may target him against bigger and more physically developed bigs.

Zhang has a terrific overlap of perimeter skill and size. He measured at 6-foot-8 (without shoes) at last year’s Nike Hoop Summit, has super soft natural shooting touch, and is a constant threat from both behind the arc and with his pull-up. While he connected on just 29% of his threes this year at Veritas, he’s been consistently higher in FIBA play. In 2024, he made 41% of his threes in the FIBA U17 World Cup and 44% in the FIBA U18 Asia Cup. He’s not just a threat to make stationary shots, but also tough ones on the move. Zhang also has a lethal pull-up game. He can put the ball on the floor with either hand, even start the break at times, and then either stop on a dime or create space with little step-backs. In either scenario, he doesn’t need much separation to get his shot off. He’s more accurate, with both his three and his pull-up, when he is balance and not drifting into his release. While he had a tendency to hunt his looks this season, there were flashes of passing and feel, that could become more valuable down the road. Physically, while Zhang has good size and solid strength, he has shorter arms and somewhat heavier feet, so it remains to be seen if he’s best defending the three or the four at the next level. He does typically rebound the ball well enough to match-up with fours, but is not an especially forceful finisher at the rim. He’s also older for his grade, having turned 19 in January of his senior season.

Adams was one of the biggest breakout players of the grassroots season, averaging 20.5 points and 11.4 rebounds per game in EYBL play while shooting 52% from the field, 42% from behind the arc, and 74% from the free-throw line. He’s a true mismatch problem who can play multiple positions and score at different levels. While Adams has a very versatile offensive attack, he has a few clear weapons. First, he’s an exceptional mid-range shooter off the dribble. He can create space with his handle, but has very advanced footwork, both at the end of his drives and in the mid-post. He’s even a threat to make some tough one-foot step backs from 12-15 feet and now starting to makes threes off the dribble. He’s also a pick-and-pop threat to the arc, able to straight-line drive bad close-outs, put the ball on the floor in the open court, and make reliable decisions (1.5 assists to 1.4 turnovers). At his best, he’s also putting pressure on the rim. At 6-foot-7 with solid length, athleticism, and cut, he’s plenty capable of operating as a big wing, but probably best suited defending opposing fours at this point. That also may be the position where his offensive versatility poses the biggest mismatch and where his rebounding is best utilized.

Wadley is a long and smooth scoring guard with a microwave type ability to put points on the board in bunches once he gets hot. He can be a tough shot-maker off the dribble and from behind the arc. He’s crafty going through the lane, athletic in open space, and able to dunk with both hands. For all his versatile weapons and fluidity, Wadley isn’t always efficient. He shot just 36% from the floor and 20% from three (on more than 6 attempts per game) in Pro16 play, showing a tendency to overcomplicate things unnecessarily and settle for tough shots. If he can simplify his approach and keep developing his feel for the game, he has the skill-set to have on-off ball versatility. Physically, he has good positional size and length, but needs to get stronger and improve his ability to play through contact. He’s a good perimeter rebounder (6.4 per game) and has a similarly good nose for the ball defensively (2.1 steals), but needs to be more physical and disciplined with his approach in order to fully maximize all his physical tools on that end of the floor. Durability is also a key factor for Wadley. He’s missed a lot of time in recent seasons with lower body injuries and just needs more game reps in order to help turn his glaring potential into more consistent production.

Spear is an intriguing long-term prospect because of his combination of physical tools and scoring versatility. At 6-foot-5 with a 6-foot-10 wingspan, he has good perimeter size and length. He’s also cut-up with broad shoulders, a strong base in his lower body, and the type of frame that could really explode in a college weight room, ultimately allowing him to play a couple of different positions. Offensively, he shows flashes of being a three-range scoring threat. He came up the ranks with the reputation as a shot-maker, but made just 26% of his threes on over 4 attempts per game in EYBL play. Still, he’s a confident shooter who looks like a projectable floor-spacer at times. He can also rise-up into mid-range pull-ups over top of contesting defenders with a high release. He’s not necessarily a dynamic handler, but is starting to utilize his size and increasing strength to make hard and physical straight-line drives to the rim in the open floor. While Spear’s skill set has some occasional inefficiencies, so too can his motor be inconsistent. When he’s playing hard and fully asserting himself though, that’s when he not only starts putting more pressure on the rim, but utilizing his physical tools to impact the game on the defensive end of the floor as well.

Collins is one of the more talented long-term prospects in the national class. At 6-foot-8, he’s long (7-foot-plus wingspan), mobile, and a very fluid mover. He covers the court extremely well, has an elastic body type, and is a good vertical athlete who can jump off either one or two feet, and then has a quick second bounce. Collins is at his best when he’s flying around the court, asserting himself on the glass (8.3 rebounds in the EYBL), being active defensively, communicating, and playing with the high motor to maximize his physical tools. As he continues to fill-out his frame, get stronger, learn the nuances of defensive angles and close-outs, there’s still significant un-tapped upside on that end of the floor. He already guards multiple positions, and has the athleticism to erase mistakes, but there’s potential for both extreme versatility on-the-ball and real playmaking ability as a help-side defender. Offensively, Collins’ game is still a work in progress. He’s best in transition and on the offensive glass. Beyond that, he has clear tools and a long-term foundation of skill, but isn’t yet ready to be an offensive focal point after averaging 12.5 points on 42% shooting from the floor and 21% from three, along with 1.5 assists versus 2.5 turnovers. He's not the type who can create his own shot on demand, but there's clear handling potential for his size. He can grab and go off the defensive glass, attack less mobile defenders to either side in open space, and go through the lane with long strides. He has soft natural touch around the paint and some long-term shooting potential, but being in rhythm is the key. He shoots a flatter ball that he often leaves short when he settles for shots that are rushed or on his heels. The bottom line is that Collins’ upside is undeniable with a terrific overlap of length, athleticism, fluidity, progressing skill, and two-way versatility. He’s a long way from a finished product though and has to build up his body, develop his offensive game, and most of all, embrace the constant high-energy style that can maximize his impact defensively, on the glass, and in his total floor game.

Felesi is a tough and strong wing who has exceptional length and plays consistently hard. His skill-set may still be a work-in-progress, but it’s trending in the right direction, and he already makes a clear impact on both ends of the floor. In fact, he’s one of the more naturally talented defenders in the class. He has an almost 7-foot wingspan, can guard multiple positions, get athletic rebounds, and snatch loose balls. Offensively, he gets downhill with force and plays in straight lines. He goes directly to the front of the rim in transition, can attack both sides in the half-court, and has the balance to absorb contact along the way. He’s a good athlete at the rim, has multiple bounces, and is a terrific offensive rebounder from the perimeter. His swing skill is his shooting. He was only 20% from behind the arc in EYBL play, but did make 73% of his free-throws. His mechanics aren’t broken when he has room and rhythm, but he has a wider base in his lower body and snaps his release a bit. He’s not much of a shooting threat off the dribble at this point though, making just 14% on dribble jumpers in EYBL play. The bottom line is that Felesi already impacts winning on both ends of the floor with his motor, competitiveness, and physical tools, and has the potential to keep improving with the progression of his guard skills.

He’s a long and athletic attacking guard who can stand out defensively, be a playmaker on that end (2.6 steals per game), and whose best offense typically comes in the open floor. He’s an excellent rebounding guard and will even fly at the offensive glass (1.7 per game) for some second chance points. Connor plays very hard and makes things happen, but can be less effective when the game slows down. He’s capable of making some open shots when he’s able to step-in, but settles for too many tough ones (23% 3pt) and can be wild at times with some of his decisions (2.6 assists vs. 1.9 turnovers).
