current_school_full: Faith Family Academy of Oak Cliff
scouting_report: Gallagher has extremely versatile two-way tools. Like his brother, he has a naturally thicker and stronger frame, but moves extremely well, giving him a lot of defensive versatility. He’s guards multiple positions, is very switchable, and has a chance to be a real standout on that end of the floor. He also has solid length and vertical athleticism, but a bit of a longer neck. He differs from his brother in two main ways. First, he’s more skilled on the perimeter. He’s a little upright off the bounce, but still capable of handling to both sides and making some plays for himself and others. He’s a capable shooter, with a notably wide base in his mechanics, but still lacks ideal consistency (32% 3pt on 3.4 attempts per game in Pro 16 play and 66% from the free-throw line). So, he offers optionality on both ends of the floor. The second area he differs from his brother is that he’s less physical, particularly in the paint. As he adds more sheer force to his game, shows an increased willingness to pressure the rim, and play through contact, his game will go to new levels.

Stevens is a budding stretch-big. He’s just growing into his body, and needs time to fill-out his frame, but he’s very skilled, smart, and plays hard. He has soft hands and touch, not just around the rim, but extending out to the arc. He shot 40% from the three-point line and 73% from the free-throw line in 10 games on the 3SSB circuit. He can even shot-fake and put the ball on the floor from the top of the key. He can post some too, with good footwork and both hands inside. He’s a good passer, who is already really intriguing playing off short rolls because he can make quick decisions and reads (1.6 assists vs. 1.3 turnovers), but also has a little floater game off the catch. Stevens is not a naturally graceful or explosive athlete, but he runs the floor and has some deceptive mobility, in large part because he competes with a motor and will battle for spots. He’s a solid shot-blocking presence (1.8 per game) and active communicator on the back-line. His rebounding could get a bit more consistent (6.2 per game), but that and his total impact should grow as he changes his body and improves his ability to play through contact. Overall, he’s a player who is trending in the right direction and playing with confidence now, but will need time to change his body before making an impact at the next level.

Ekezie is a true 7-footer with long arms and vertical athleticism. At the 2025 NBPA Top 100 Camp, he measured at 7-foot-1 with a 7-foot-4.5 wingspan and 9-foot-4.5 standing reach. He’s a bouncy finisher, who frequently dunks on the way up, and a real rim protector who averaged over 2 blocks per game in both the E16 circuit with Vegas Elite and the high school season with Southeastern Prep. His most immediate impact at the college level will be as a vertical threat and finisher, shot-blocker, and drop coverage ball-screen defender. Ekezie is still young though, turning 18 in May of 2026, and has tools to develop in other areas. He has a projectable frame that should continue to add muscle mass and really blossom in a college weight room, but still needs to develop the physicality and assertiveness to maximize it. He also has a foundation for further skill development with a soft natural touch, good left hand, and signs of passing potential. Physically, he gets off his feet quickly, can run the floor, and even make a straight-line close-out, but does have tighter hips that can be problematic when moving laterally. Consequently, he lacks defensive versatility in that he’s rarely switchable or able to guard ball-screens in more than one way.

He is a legit jumbo guard who is standing every bit of 6-foot-6, if not 6-foot-7, and is an exceptionally fluid mover. He sees the floor well, is a quality passer, has good use of both hands, advanced early footwork, a clean shooting stroke, and some playmaking. While his frame is still largely undeveloped, his athleticism has started to come on in recent years and he has all the tools to be an impactful defender with his size, length, and court coverage. For all his tools, Johnston is not yet a finished product. His overall impact on the game doesn’t yet come in consistent correlation to his natural talent. In addition to developing his body, he has to learn where and when to best assert himself, but the long-term upside is undeniable.

Edema is a major physical presence in the lane. He stands at just under 7-feet, has an already powerful frame, no problem throwing his body around, and solid athleticism around both rims. He's capable of making an immediate impact at the next level as a post defender, rebounder, solid shot-blocker, screener, and lob threat. He could stand to be a quicker finisher at times, but can convert emphatically when he has the space and time to power up. The Nigerian native has also seen his fundamental skills make tremendous strides in the years he's played at Cushing Academy. Initially, his hands were a major concern as he struggled to come up with clean catches and make decisions with the ball. Now, although he's not a shooting threat, he can step to the perimeter to be ball-reversal option or play out of dribble hand-offs. His defensive polish and decisions have made similar strides and should ease his transition to the next level.

Kayil combines good backcourt size, strength, skill level, and feel for the game. He measures in at over 6-foot-5 with shoes on and has built up his body with noticeable muscle mass. He lacks great length or true explosiveness, but he’s versatile in offensive structure because he’s a confident shooter and has on/off ball versatility. He’s a high-volume shooter from behind the arc who has clean mechanics and deep range both off the catch and the dribble. He played primarily off the ball with the German U19 World Cup team, but gets to operate with the ball in his hands with Alba Berlin. He will get the team into their offense and get the ball moving, but isn’t really a dynamic creator or someone who can consistently collapse a defense. He can also struggle to finish at the rim at times. At the college level, he projects more as a secondary handler who can play out of ball-screens and make reads on the second side of the floor, without having to be the focal point of opposing defenses pressure. Defensively, he’s consistently generated a high steal rate, and has the size and strength to be valuable. He’ll be limited by his lack of length and ideal foot-speed. While he turns 20 years old in January, the speed and physicality of the college game could be an adjustment for him on that end of the floor as well.

Rippey is an explosively athletic lead guard who can set the tone on the defensive end of the floor. He pressures the ball, picks up the length of the floor, gets over ball-screens, and is ideal at the point of attack. While there could be times down the road where his lack of size and length could be targeted on that end, that's strictly theoretical right now. Offensively, Rippey is a dynamic athlete with the ball, both in terms of his burst into the lane and ability to rise up for explosive dunks. That explosiveness allows him to make momentum-changing plays at virtually any point and also consistently pressure the rim. Simultaneously, he's made significant strides with his shooting. He made 34% of his attempts from behind the arc in 3SSB play, but has shown further progress during his senior season. He has developed very clean mechanics in rhythm and is just starting to make tougher shots with range now. Another area where we've seen steady growth with Rippey is in his ability to run the team and be totally reliable from the point guard position. He has all the competitive intangibles a coach could want at that spot and has gradually been showing a more in-depth understanding of the nuances of making reads and decisions with the ball. Like many dynamic athletes, Rippey can assert himself in spurts, and so there have been times in the past when he could be a little suspect with his decisions (3.1 turnovers) or shot-selection (38% FG). The bottom line is that Rippey's defense and athleticism separate him, and he has the motor to maximize both. He's also a worker who has cut up his frame and has established a track record of consistently improving his game.

Huan is a developing southpaw big man with terrific long-term tools, but still putting them all together in order to be consistently productive. He has good size, coordination, soft hands, touch, mobility, and can run the floor well. He’s not a finished product physically, but his frame is sturdy and should have no problem supporting plenty more muscle mass. He averaged five blocked shots per game in the FIBA U19 World Cup (1.2 in EYBL play) thanks to his combination of size and mobility. Offensively, he has touch around the rim, is comfortable with the ball on the perimeter (despite a high turnover rate), and has long-term floor-spacing potential despite shooting a bit of a moonball. He can disappear at times though and go long stretches without making his presence felt. He isn’t much of a post presence at this point and is somewhat reliant on others to get him going. The other area of his game that is far too inconsistent is his rebounding. He definitely needs to get stronger, but more than that, just show an increased nose for the ball and willingness to pursue it outside his area. While Huan is undoubtedly a work in progress, he’s a seven-foot southpaw with extreme mobility, shot-blocking tools, and versatile two-way potential.

Rogers is a skilled guard with a mature and polished game. He’s not a naturally dynamic athlete, but he’s worked to build up his body and is well-rounded with the ball. His handle is tight, so that while he may not blow by his defender with his first-step, he can eventually separate. He has a wide array of pull-ups and floaters, with a pretty high release and occasional fade when necessary. He’s a below the rim finisher in traffic, but has touch and a willingness to play through contact that gets him to the free-throw line consistently. Rogers has been thrust into being a primary creator recently, and lost some efficiency in that role, averaging 17.5 points on 36% shooting and 3.4 assists versus 3 turnovers in Pro16 play. Long-term though, his on/off ball versatility and feel for the game are important assets. He’s a better floor-spacer than the numbers show (29% three-point), very adept at attacking close-outs, a willing ball-mover and solid passer once the dominos start falling. Defensively, he’s not going to overwhelm anyone, but should be reliable with the acumen to be in the right spots and a willingness to be physical.

Foster is a long and smooth scoring wing. His most notable early weapons came with his mid-range pull-up game or when he was able to get out in the open floor. More recently though, we’ve seen him begin to establish his three-point shooting as a true weapon. He knocked down 39% of his attempts from behind the arc in EYBL play, including 42% on unguarded catch and shoot threes. His range off the dribble is also now extending beyond the arc as well. For all of his recent growth, Foster is still a long way from a finished product. He’s long, with a 6-foot-9-plus wingspan, and still relatively lean. As he continues to build up his body he’ll be able to play through more contact and potentially even continue to get more explosive. While he has some budding handle and can rise-up for some big dunks when he gets loose in transition, he doesn’t always separate or pressure the rim as much against a set half-court defense just yet. Another area for potential growth is on the defensive end of the floor. He has the length, size, and fluidity to be effective, but needs to be more physical and show a little more sense of urgency and fight on that end of the floor. The bottom line is that Foster is a multi-dimensional scorer who has consistently added new weapons to his game, and yet continues to have a lot of room for future growth as he continues to get stronger and more consistently assertive.

Carter-Givens isn’t close to being a finished product yet, and has to get significantly stronger, but has a wealth of versatile tools that could make him a long-term sleeper. He’s a long and athletic big with broad shoulders who is already a major rim protector (2.4 blocks per game) and efficient finisher at the rim (71% FG). He runs the floor well, is mobile for his size, makes tough catches, is a multi-bounce leaper, lob threat, and on the offensive glass for tip-dunks and tip-ins. The biggest limitation right now is just a lack of strength. He’s lean in his lower body and through his chest, so he can get bumped off his spots on both ends of the floor, despite rarely shying away from contact. Simultaneously though, there are also offensive limitations (5.1 points per game), particularly away from the basket. He’s not much of a scoring threat beyond the rim, nor a completely reliable decision-maker on the perimeter (0.6 assists vs. 1.2 turnovers). He could also stand to be a higher-volume rebounder (5.6 per game), particularly on the defensive end.

Wade is a long and lanky big man who is athletic and very active. He has a lean build with high shoulders, long legs, and a high center of gravity that can make it difficult to maintain his positioning at times. He’s also a bow-legged mover, but still runs and changes ends very well. He’s equally bouncy and also plays an energetic style with unusual endurance for a big man. Wade’s primary asset is his shot-blocking, as he contests or changes virtually everything around him. He blocked 3.3 shots per game in UAA play, but has to get more disciplined with his positioning and when to leave his feet. Still, he can erase mistakes when trailing the ball or coming from the weakside of the floor and is also a good defensive communicator. That propensity to leave his feet though, combined with his difficulty holding his ground through contact, can limit his effectiveness as a defensive rebounder. Offensively though, he’s very active going to the glass for tip-ins (2.9 offensive rebounds per game) and an extremely efficient finisher at the rim (77%). He also has some long-term touch to develop facing the basket, albeit with elongated face-up mechanics that aren’t consistent right now (24% 3pt on 2.6 attempts per game & 58% FT). The bottom line is that Wade has clear physical tools and potential, along with the motor to maximize them, but is still working on strength and some fundamentals on both ends of the floor. He’s also older for his grade and will turn 19 in March of his senior year.

Toure is a powerful athlete on the wing who is an absolute wrecking ball going to the rim. He’s dynamic in the open floor, has a good first-step in the half-court, and can bully his way through contact like few others in the class. He’s a truly explosive leaper, who rises up with extreme quickness and power, and a violent finisher who can immediately change the momentum of a game with a single dunk. While he can be physically dominant, his ball skills and overall feel for the game have shown consistent growth as well. He’s gradually learning to pick his spots more efficiently as a driver, can rise-up into short pull-ups, and is starting to show a developing passing instinct. His three-ball, which is more of a set shot, remains more of a work in progress. He is a good wing rebounder (6.6 per game), has all the physical tools to be a lockdown defender, and already has good playmaking metrics with 3 stocks per game (1.6 steals and 1.4 turnovers).

Collingwood is trending towards a strong and athletic wing with evolving skill. Initially, he was almost more of a hybrid four, who thrived with his aggression, physicality, and motor. Then his shooting and perimeter skill-set improved, but he was inefficient trying too hard to prove it. This summer we saw everything come together. He attacked downhill in the open floor and exploded for big finishes. He rose-up to knock down pull-ups and threes, because he was on balance with his legs under him. Maintaining that same approach, and prioritizing efficiency while showing his expanding skill-set is the key to him continuing to rise. That includes shot-selection (36% FG), decision-making (0.9 assists vs 1.5 turnovers), and his willingness to move the ball. Defensively, he has the potential to be similarly versatile, and extremely switchable, given his overlap of strength and mobility. He’s already good in passing lanes (1.5 steals), but could be more active on the glass (2.9).

Ross is a high upside prospect who is already a tremendous shot-blocker and still growing into both his body and offensive game. He’s a springy athlete who is mobile and quick off the ground vertically. He’s a terrific rim protector coming from the weakside of the floor, but he can block shots on the ball as well. He’s fairly switchable, not just because he can move his feet on the perimeter, but because he stays in the play, even if initially beat, and can block shots when trailing the play. Offensively, he’s a bouncy finisher who dunks ball on the way up and is a lob threat. He’s still working to build up his body though and not yet able to play through contact as consistently as he will need to at the next level. Ross also has a soft touch, not just around the rim, but with some long-term shooting potential. While he was not a good free-throw shooter during the Pro16 season (49%), he did make 33% of his threes on nearly 2 attempts per game. The bottom line is that Ross is an immediate impact defender who could just be scratching the surface of his long-term potential if he can keep adding muscle, getting more physical, and ultimately be someone who spaces the floor both vertically and with his shooting.

Ogden is bigger-bodied scoring wing who put up 20 points per game on the 3SSB circuit on 47% shooting from the floor, 46% from behind the arc, and 87% from the free-throw line. He took over 7 threes per game and shot 49% on catch-and-shoot attempts, including a whopping 58% when he was unguarded. While the long-distance shooting has evolved into his biggest weapon, he’s far from a one-dimensional threat. He’s crafty, can take smaller defenders into the mid-post, and has the high release to score over top of them with his pull-up. He’s also tough, physical, and has a real knack for getting himself to the free-throw line. He’s making strides as a handler, passer, and decision-maker, but can be turnover prone at times (3.1 assists vs. 3.2 turnovers). He can still lack some quickness and length with his first-step, but is also gradually putting more pressure on the rim. Physically, he was a bit of an early bloomer, who was bigger and stronger than most of his peers at a younger age. His quickness and athleticism have improved though. He’s finishing plays well above the rim in the open floor, more of a legitimate wing on the defensive end, and should fill-out nicely in a college weight room. He’s also an excellent perimeter rebounder (5.8 per game). Most importantly, he’s improving at a clearly accelerated rate and seems to have a real drive to win.

Cloer is a big-time shooter of the basketball who also possesses good wing size and bounce at the rim. He has a high and compact release, gets beautiful loft and rotation on his ball, and is a threat to make all different types of shots. He can make threes on the move or off the dribble, knocked down 43% of his open spot-up threes in EYBL play, and also has a complimenting pull-up game with his ability to score overtop of most opposing wings. Most of all, he has that outward confidence and swagger that almost all great shot-makers share. While Cloer isn’t really a creator off the dribble, he has a defined niche that is translatable to the highest levels and a good complimenting skill-set. He uses his shot-fake very well, has a long first step if he gets his defender in the air, good feel as a ball mover, a solid left hand, and can take smaller defenders into the post. He’s even shown some flashes of being able to be a secondary pick-and-roll threat. Physically, he has very good positional size at over 6-foot-7 (with shoes) and the bounce to be a lob threat or rise-up for explosive open floor finishes. While he lacks ideal length and has a bit of a long neck, he’s gotten stronger and added some noticeable muscle mass definition. Defensively, increased strength and physicality will only benefit him, but there is room for growth with his footspeed and attention to detail at times.

Andrews is a big and physical scoring wing. Measuring in at 6-foot-7 (in shoes) with a very long 7-foot wingspan and powerful frame, he is physically imposing in a multitude of ways and plays with the attacking style to magnify those assets. He gets downhill with extreme force, absorbs contact, and can rise-up for emphatic finishes in the open floor. He was a bit of an early bloomer, who was bigger and stronger than most of his peers at an early age, and consequently able to thrive with a bully ball type style. More recently though, he's gradually adding more skill and nuance to his game. He’s making strides with his shooting. There is still a hitch in his release at times, which is ironically most visible at the free-throw line, but his mechanics look more fluid from behind the arc as he’s learning to pick his spots and proving he’s capable of knocking down open rhythm shots (36% 3pt & 72% FT). The next order of business in continuing to develop his right hand. Defensively, he has all the physical characteristics to be impactful and already a high steal rate (1.8 per game). Right now, he's more versatile switching up the line-up though and can give up some straight-line drives when matched up with opposing guards on the perimeter.

Mingo is a big guard with length, versatility, natural feel for the game, and an ability to get wherever he wants with the ball in his hands. He has a tight handle, can break his defender down with combo-moves, navigate a crowded lane instinctively, make reads out of ball-screens, utilize both hands, and create for himself and others. He is also a terrific perimeter rebounder (7.6 per game), very disruptive defender (2 steals), and simultaneously capable of guarding multiple positions. While he has very good size and length at over 6-foot-4 with a better than 6-foot-10 wingspan, he’s a bit bow-legged and thus doesn’t always cover the court with a smooth gate. He can occasionally be nonchalant with the ball, which contributes to a relatively high turnover rate (2.8 assists vs. 2.5 turnovers), but the biggest swing skill is his shooting. He made just 23% of his threes in 13 EYBL games and has somewhat unorthodox mechanics, beginning with his catch and load into the shot. Mingo has been a well-known prospect since he was younger but has continued to ascend with each passing year. That rate of improvement, as well as his young age (he won’t turn 17 until October of his senior year) suggests that there could still be significant untapped potential as he continues to climb the ranks in the national class.

Alozie is a strong and athletic lead guard. He has elite burst with the ball in his hands, combining extreme speed and power to get downhill like few other guards in the country. He accelerates out of his moves off the dribble, can attack both sides, has a knack for attacking his defender’s back in transition, and is consistently able to drive through contact. He’s a threat to finish explosively above the rim or with touch and body control. He has solid positional size, long arms (6-6 wingspan), and plays even bigger than he is, often zipping passes over the defense. Alozie’s length, physicality, and decisive movements also make him a value defender, particularly at the point of attack where he can pressure the ball and pick-up for the length of the floor (1.4 steals per game in the EYBL). He is a very good rebounding guard (6.3 per game), who loves to bust out and look to create tempo on demand. While he’s made progress over the years, both his decision making and shooting can be inconsistent at times. He has good elevation and rotation on a clean release when he’s in rhythm, but can spray some shots when he’s rushed or on the move (75% FT but 26% 3pt). He also has a bit of a high dribble at times and had a particularly alarming turnover rate during the recent EYBL season (2.6 assists vs. 3.5 turnovers per game). Alozie is going to have a much easier time than most incoming freshman adjusting to the physicality of the college game, but he’s closer to a finished product physically, so whatever untapped upside there is will have to come through the continued progression of his ball skills and mastering the nuances of the point guard position.

Rueckert is a skilled shooter with good wing size at 6-foot-8. He shot 36% from behind the arc in EYBL play, on just under 7 attempts per game, but was a whopping 47% on unguarded catch-and-shoot threes. He has supreme gravity as a spot-up floor spacer, but is also a movement shooter who can make threes off screens. His release is compact and extremely repeatable, beginning with very good balance in his lower body. The threat of Ruckert’s shooting also opens up the rest of his attack. While he may not have a dynamic first-step against set defenders, he’s a fluid ball-handler, very adept at using subtle shot fakes, able to drive both sides with an advanced left hand, and then be crafty in the lane. He’s also an underrated vertical athlete when he can elevate off two feet, and has sneaky bounce jumping off his right leg for a right-handed player. As he continues to build-up his body, improve his ability to absorb contact, and maintain the line of his drives, his game will go to new levels. His size is going to allow him to play multiple positions offensively, but he lacks great length and isn't especially elastic or bendy in his upper body. He has the basketball IQ and awareness to be a reliable team defender, but needs to establish a primary position on that end of the floor, either by be consistently keeping smaller and quicker wings in front of him or by being able to rebound (3.5) and hold his ground against opposing fours. Overall, his shooting, skill, and potential to flourish in offensive structure gives him an obvious niche at the next level, while his ceiling will be determined by the gains he can make with his creation, physical strength, and defensive impact.

Diop is a physically gifted big man. He stands at 6-foot-10 with shoes on, has a long 7-foot-2 wingspan, and is a high-level run and jump athlete, particularly for his size. He excels at changing ends of the floor and has immediate value as a rim-runner and lob threat. He’s also cut-up with a frame that should only continue to fill-out and add muscle mass in a college weight room. While Diop has glaring physical tools, he’s older for his grade (turning 19 in November of his senior year) and didn’t produce to the level expected during the recent grassroots season. He averaged 7 points, 5 rebounds, and less than a block per game in 3SSB play. It was the lack of rebounding and shot-blocking that was particularly concerning. He has all the tools to be effective in those areas, as well as enough mobility to eventually have some versatility guarding ball-screens. His offensive game is a known work in progress, so maximizing that defensive impact is critical to both his long-term success and chances to making an immediate impact at the next level. On the other end, he’s shown some growth with his footwork, dexterity, and even has some touch to develop long-term (80% FT in 3SSB play), but his hands are still developing and he can struggle to score at times.

Jackson is a power wing who brings strength, physicality, rim pressure, and defensive intensity. He’s built almost like a football player with a broad and powerful upper body. He gets downhill with the ball extremely well, attacking with lots of sheer force in straight lines. He’s particularly effective in the open court or coming off a pitch in the half-court. His physically imposing, attacking style also extends to the defensive end of the floor where he is aggressive, physical, able to move his feet, and switchable. While Jackson’s physicality and aggressive two-way style is his defining attribute, we’ve seen him balance out those characteristics with more nuances in his game as he’s matured. He’s navigating the lane much better, spinning off contact, rarely running people over, and learning to slow down and play at different speeds. Still though, his decision-making with the ball can be inconsistent as he had a very high turnover rate (3.4 per game) in EYBL play. His shooting remains another key long-term swing skill. He made 30% of his threes and 61% of his free-throws in EYBL play. Mechanically, his stroke has made strides and is gradually getting more capable with room and rhythm, to the point where he can no longer be deemed a total non-shooter.

Olajuwon is a big wing who can defend and make threes. He’s every bit of 6-foot-6 and a bit of a late-bloomer who has a sturdy build, but is still filling out and blossoming athletically. He shot 40% from behind the arc, including 49% on open catch-and-shoot threes, this year in the EYBL. He has good rotation and a compact release, but tends to twist on his way up, which is perhaps why his ball can get a little harder when he’s on the move or shooting off the dribble. Nevertheless though, he has extreme gravity and plenty of potential to keep diversifying his shot profile. Where he’s a little more limited is with his creation. He spaces the floor and plays off the threat of his jumper, but isn’t going to break many defenders down off the dribble. Instead, he can attack close-outs in straight lines, get to his pull-up, or go through the lane with long strides. He’s disciplined with his shot selection and has shown some passing flashes within the flow of the offense (1.7 assists vs. 1.7 turnovers). Defensively, he’s solid with the potential to keep getting more versatile as he builds up his body. He’s already willing to be physical and bang a little bit inside. He has pretty naturally quick reactions and good hands (1.4 steals and 0.7 blocks per game in EYBL), but there are other moments when he can look a tad stiff on that end. Olajuwon is also a very good wing rebounder (5.9 per game).

Karvala is loaded with glaring upside. He’s a big, nearly 6-foot-7 wing with a pretty shooting stroke from downtown. He runs well, covers the court, and can rise-up explosively to play above the rim. The key is putting it all together consistently and efficiently in order to impact winning to the highest degree. While this was a breakout grassroots season for Karvala, he averaged 14 points per game on just 38% shooting from the floor and 34% from behind the arc, while attempting roughly two-third of his total from three. There’s no question that Karvala is a projectable shooter. He has a smooth release and showed versatility in his shot-making – on the move, running to the arc in transition, and being particularly good off the dribble. There were times though when his shot could get a little flat or when he could disappear altogether for stretches and not impact the game quite as much as someone with his talent should. As he continues to adjust to playing against high-level competition, he needs to learn to assert himself consistently and find other ways to impact the game when he isn’t getting looks from downtown. Simultaneously, he has to build up his body, finish through contact more consistently at the rim, defend with consistent competitive physicality, and improve his decision-making abilities with the ball (0.8 assists vs. 2.2 turnovers). The bottom line though is that he has extreme tools and could be in the very early stages of putting them all together.

At 6-foot-7, Morillo combines perimeter size with toughness and a very high basketball IQ. He's multi-positional, able to initiate like a big guard, and make plays for himself and others with physicality and passing acumen. He seeks out and absorbs contact off the dribble, draws a lot of fouls in the process, but is simultaneously crafty enough to read the floor while doing it. He has the versatility to play both on and off the ball, and can even take smaller defenders into the post, but his feel for the game is a constant. His competitive streak also extends to the defensive end of the floor, where's he's often under-rated. He's not the type of defender who is going to pick-up and turn opposing ball-handlers for the length of the floor, but when he's not over-extended, he's able to get into their bodies, pressure the ball with his physicality, and keep them in front. He's also switchable up the line-up onto bigger players and a tremendous perimeter rebounder who loves to take the ball off the glass and start the break himself. The two most glaring limitations in Morillo's game are a lack of dynamic explosiveness and consistent three-point shooting. He made just 20% of his three-point attempts in EYBL play, even while being very selective with his attempts (just 1.5 of his 13.3 field goal attempts were from three), and was 60% from the free-throw line. He's capable of making mid-range pull-ups, but his release gets more elongated with a noticeable hitch as he gets deeper. Athletically, he lacks overwhelming natural burst and has somewhat shorter arms.

Pearson is a throwback pure point guard who runs the team and distributes the ball at a very high level. He has great vision, feel for the game, and pace with the ball in his hands. He's extremely smooth, can whip passes off the dribble, and deliver balls with both hands. He has solid positional size, but isn't a totally dynamic athlete, and needs to be a more reliable three-point shooter. He converted 30% from behind the arc in EYBL play and has a slight hitch in his release. Defensively, he's very solid guarding the ball and also has good hands (2.2 steals per game). The bottom line is that he's a college ready floor general with room to keep improving.

scouting_report: Washington is one of the highest-level athletes in the national class. He’s an extreme leaper who makes it look almost effortless when he gets his head on the rim. He can rise-up to catch lobs, fly at the offense glass for tip-dunks (7.2 rebounds per game in UAA play, including 2.6 on the offensive end), or throw down the type of open floor dunks that can immediately change the momentum of a game. Washington is also a super smooth runner, who sprints the floor with long, fluid strides. He has terrific wing size with long arms and long legs, but that high center of gravity and lean build makes it difficult for him to hold his ground, even though he’s not averse to contact. He has some corresponding defensive upside with his length, twitchy reactions (1.1 steals), court coverage potential, and perimeter shot-blocking (1.1 blocks), but is nonetheless still early in the process of being disciplined and efficient with his angles, footwork, and technique. It’s a similar story offensively, where he has obvious talent with his physical gifts and even some touch to develop, but has to improve his decision-making and shot-selection (37% FG and 1.3 assists vs. 1.8 turnovers). He needs to be a more willing ball-mover, make quicker decisions with the ball, establish reliable ways to create his own offense in the half-court, and finish better around the rim when he’s unable to dunk (38% on lay-ups). He’s also older for his grade and will turn 19 in December of his senior year. After a somewhat underwhelming grassroots season, we've some more recent signs of progress during the high school season, with more efficient decisions and some gains in his spot-up shooting. Continued progression will be key for one of the most physically gifted prospects in the class as he, hopefully, continues to turn potential into production on a more consistent basis.

Oumiddoch is a skilled volume scorer. He has good positional size, but lacks ideal strength or athleticism, and compensates by being ultra-crafty. He has great hands and naturally soft touch, so while his mechanics are unorthodox, he’s historically been a shot-maker. He’s a confident handler, but lacks a super explosive first-step, so he can over-dribble at times in search of chances to utilize his cleverness. He also has an assortment of lay-ups at his disposal. Historically, he’s been a high-volume player who could score in spurts when he got going, but couldn’t easily acclimate to more structure or less offensive volume. This grassroots season though, he benefitted from being a secondary creator, and play with newfound efficiency, averaging 17 points on 49% shooting, 38% from three, and 86% from the free-throw line. Sustaining, and even leaning into, that approach, will be key to transitioning his game to the next level. Then it becomes a matter of building up his body and improving defensively. He can be flat-footed guarding the ball and tends to just stand-up and ball-watch from the weakside of the floor. Physically, he’s thin and not ready to play through the level of contact he’s going to see at the next level.

Johnson is a scoring lead guard who is constantly hunting his individual offense. The southpaw has a deep bag to rely on and is capable of creating on demand from multiple levels. He’s smooth, quick, and creative with his handle, particularly when he has room to operate. He can euro through traffic when playing in the open floor or rock his man before breaking him down in the half-court. He also has a full complement of mid-range weapons with floaters, pull-ups, and step-backs. He shoots a naturally soft ball, but has a lower release point that comes up from his chest, and may be more comfortable shooting off the dribble than the catch at this point. He can also be crafty at the rim, but only finishes 49% of his lay-up attempts in EYBL play, as he can get swallowed up when getting too deep into traffic. While Johnson could stand to get more consistent with his three-point shooting off the catch and continue to add to his finishing package, the biggest adjustment upon reaching the next level will be adapting to less volume. While he’s a plenty talented passer, he’s ball dominant right now, looking to score first and foremost, and then typically passing out of necessity. Some of that is contextual on an Indy Heat team that needed him to score, and he did show growth down the stretch of the season with his decision-making and defensive commitment, but they’ll both need to continue. Maximizing his physical tools will also be key. He has broad shoulders and long arms, but has a wiry and relatively lean build right now, and also isn’t a totally dynamic athlete at the rim.

Bryant has some glaring physical tools and signs of developing two-way versatility, but is still in the relatively early stages of putting it all together, despite being older for his grade (he turns 19 in November of his senior year). He’s athletic, mobile, has good positional size, and typically plays with the motor necessary to maximize those tools. He runs the floor very well, gets above the rim with ease, can jump off on or two feet, is an emphatic finisher, major lob threat, and active offensive rebounder (2.1 per game in Pro 16 play). He’s even put on noticeable muscle mass in recent months. Then there are flashes of inside-out tools. He’s willing to post, can go through the lane with a single bounce, and has some developing face-up touch. The problem is he can try too hard to prove that skill and is rarely a quick decision-maker on the perimeter, so he can be a ball-stopper and not always as outright productive as his tools suggest (13.3 points and 5.8 rebounds in Pro 16). Defensively, it’s a similar story. His size, mobility, quickness off the floor contesting shots, and ability to cover the court give him obvious potential, but his positioning, decisions, and consequent reliability are a work in progress.

Paul is a point forward type and jumbo initiator. At 6-foot-7-plus with a long 7-foot wingspan and naturally thicker build, he’s an extremely fluid handler and good passer for his size. He can take the ball off the glass to start the break himself, advance it up the floor with the dribble, and then create for himself and others in the half-court (3.8 assists vs. 2.5 turnovers in 8 UAA games). He can string together combo moves, has a good floater game, and is nearly ambidextrous from 8-10 feet in, as well as with his passing. He also has excellent natural hands, so while he isn’t an overly explosive vertical athlete and needs to get more comfortable playing through contact in the lane, he compensates by being a very quick finisher. The fit at the next level is going to be very important, because Paul needs the ball in his hands to be effective right now, as well as specific types of defensive match-ups. He’s a capable shooter off the dribble, but very reluctant and inconsistent off the catch (55% FT & 23% 3pt). Defensively, he shows spurts of using his length, but is best defending slower-footed fours at this point. He’s battled injuries and could benefit from sculpting his frame, but has shown he can be a high-volume rebounder (8.5 per game).

Burnett is volume scorer and playmaker who operates with extreme offensive freedom and a corresponding amount of style and flair. He’s streaky bucket getter who can put points on the board in bunches when he gets rolling. He has a ton of handle. He will rock his defender with combo moves until he ultimately shakes him, be as elusive with his footwork at the end of his drives, and has an advanced left hand. While his mechanics are far from pure, he’s a tough shot-maker who leans back into a high release. He can be a little ball dominant and needs to keep getting more efficient, but he’s the type of guard who can go to work and create on demand. Burnett isn’t known for his passing just yet, but he can see the floor and make quality reads when he’s willing. Defensively too, he shows flashes of being productive with long arms and a good nose for the ball, but just needs to commit to being consistently disciplined. Physically, he has good size at 6-foot-4, a rangy build with long arms and an elastic type frame.

Caldwell is long, strong, and a physical force inside. He hunts the rim and finishes emphatically at every opportunity. He can face-up to get downhill off the dribble, play in the post, or out of the dunker spot. He’s also an active offensive rebounder. With his aggressive style, he’s capable of playing the four or the five, but doesn’t offer much rim protection (0.1 blocks per game in 3SSB play) and is a little livelier on the offensive glass than defensive (4.2 defensive rebounds per game). He’s also working to expand his skill-set away from the paint (53% FT, 18% three-point, and 0.3 assists vs. 1.7 turnovers in 3SSB play).

scouting_report: Placide is a big, strong, and mobile forward who stands out first and foremost with his defensive versatility. He’s multi-positional on that end with the size and strength to bang in the post and the footspeed to switch onto guards and slide his feet. He’s a good vertical athlete who can finish hard above the rim and has multiple bounces in the lane. That helps him to be an excellent rebounder on both ends of the floor (8.8 per game in Pro 16 play, including 3.5 on the offensive end). Offensively, he puts a lot of pressure on the rim, both as a driver and a roller. He likes to put the ball on the floor, but it’s been the increasingly physical downhill style that has made him more effective as he seeks out, and plays through, contact. Sometimes that can go too far, and he can crash into bodies, but he has some agility to work with. Similarly, there are times he can try to do too much, but he’s capable of starting the break himself, using both hands, and passing off the dribble. He’s also very adept at turning drives into post-ups, which is a subtle, but important, aspect of his game. Placide’s biggest swing skill is his shooting. Right now, he’s a non-shooter who made just 39% of his free-throws and was 1-14 from behind the arc in 13 league games. He has decent touch though and so if he can ever smooth out his mechanics and evolve into even a capable floor-spacer, it changes how he can be utilized on that end. Working on that aspect of his game, while continuing to embrace his newfound physicality and not sacrificing his efficiency by drifting to the perimeter too much, is at the sweat spot of taking his game to new levels.

- Two-sport star who dominated Chicago Public Schools competition on the gridiron and hardwood for four years at the prep level. Could realistically play on either side of the ball at the next level, but projects best as a slot receiver. - Verified a shade over 5-foot-10, 161 pounds with a +4 wingspan at the 2026 Navy All-American Bowl. - Possesses dunk contest bounce and high-flying open-floor finishing ability on the court that translates via ladder-climbing acrobatic snags at the edge of his catch radius and explosive cuts before and after the catch. - Did not compete on the elite offseason football camp/7-on-7 circuit, but more than proved his own at the NAAB following his senior season. - Best element of his game at this stage is the ability to make defenders whiff in the open field and in a phone booth. - Will need to commit serious time and effort across multiple offseasons before his play strength is adequate at the top levels of the college game. - Major box-checker off the field as he plays and prepares with an ideal competitive temperament, and is a player teammates naturally gravitate towards. - Legendary CPS two-sport resume paired with promising in-person live exposure point and athletic upside could manifest in Rankin eventually ending up on NFL Draft radars. More Evaluations Evaluated 04/02/2025 Hudson Standish 247Sports National Analyst Read

Sheffield is a major physical presence in the lane. Measuring in at 6-foot-9 (with shoes) and 240 pounds, he’s built with powerful legs, strong shoulders, and a naturally thicker frame, with still plenty of room to add additional muscle mass. He plays with a motor, seeks out contact, and has a physical style on both ends of the floor, but is also a good athlete. He can get off his feet vertically at the rim, run the floor well, and even has some agility and lateral mobility for his size. While he’s not necessarily a high-volume scorer, he has a translatable offensive role as a rim-runner, finisher out of the dunker spot, big screener, and offensive rebounder. He sprints into ball screens, presents a big target rolling through the lane, and has solid hands. He should be a threat to duck-in for deep post catches, where he has some footwork inside, good balance and lift through contact. He isn’t much of a scoring or shooting threat away from the basket though, and needs to be a much more consistent free-throw shooter after converting just 39% in EYBL play. Defensively, he’s a deterrent in the lane with his intimidating presence, a quality post defender who can absorb contact in his chest without getting displaced, and a solid shot-blocker (1.1 per game in EYBL play), despite lacking great length. He’s mobile enough to play in various ball-screen coverages, but needs to do a better job of finishing possessions on the glass.

Smith is a wide-bodied mismatch forward. He has a broad build, high shoulders, long arms (7-foot-1 wingspan), and soft hands. While he’s built to create space inside, he’s actually most comfortable facing up and playing off his dribble. He can create with both hands, and while he doesn’t blow by many defenders, he dictates his own pace, has great balance through contact, and is very difficult to get around once he establishes his angle. Whether it’s off the dribble or the catch, he has good instincts at the rim and is a lay-up maker from various angles (69% at the rim in UAA play), although he could raise his release point in the lane at times. Smith may not be much of a shooter (57% FT & 18% 3pt), but he is a reliable decision-maker with the ball, who can facilitate from various spots on the floor. He can start the break himself, hit cutters from the block or high post, play out of dribble hand-off action, and has clear potential to play out of short-rolls at the next level. Smith is a bit unconventional, so it’s important that he goes to a situation that allows him to play to his strengths. The fit will also be important defensively, because at 6-foot-6, he isn’t twitchy or super quick, but also lacks the bounce to be a real shot-blocking threat (0.3 per game in UAA play). He compensates by being physical inside and a high-volume rebounder (7.7 per game).

The 6-foot-5 lefty is a limited athlete who plays a bit upright on both ends of the floor, but compensates with tremendous feel for the game, floor vision, and elite passing ability (5.8 assists per game in Pro16 play). As a scorer, he has soft natural touch that extends to the arc (38% 3pt), but is more limited in the lane (42% 2pt FG). His inability to separate or get above the rim can result in him settling for tough twos off his back foot. Defensively, he needs the right matchup because of his lack of footspeed, but he’s strong, very alert, active with his hands (2.2 steals and 0.6 blocks per game), reliably in the right spots both on and off the ball, and a good rebounder (6.9 per game). The bottom line is that Sancomb has clear physical limitations, but stuffs all columns of the stat sheet and seems to have the type of instincts and aptitude for the game that can’t be taught.

Stokes is the most talented prospect in the national class and a unique match-up for opposing defenders. At 6-foot-7 and 230 pounds with a 7-foot wingspan, he’s powerful, long, and explosively athletic. But while he’s built like a forward, he can make plays like a guard with an ability to create off the dribble and an innate understanding of how to instinctively find a path to the rim, even when one doesn’t seem to initially present itself. He’s especially lethal getting downhill in the open floor and loves to take the ball off the defensive glass (10 rebounds per game in EYBL) and start the break himself. Because Stokes can be so physically imposing, it’s easy for some of the nuances in his game to go under-appreciated. But he has terrific hands, allowing him to hold on to anything he touches and simultaneously have good touch. He also has very good footwork, both on the perimeter and at the end of his drives, and even in the mid-post. Stokes is also a much better passer than he often gets credit for, so while his turnover rate can be high (3.8 assists to 3.6 turnovers), he has real vision and skill setting up his teammates when he’s not being nonchalant with the ball. The shooting is his biggest offensive swing skill, and while he has made notable strides over the years, he still has a tendency to drop his left shoulder and shoot down on the ball a bit. Nevertheless, he still shot 36% from behind the arc on over 3 attempts per game. Defensively, he has all the physical tools and good playmaking metrics (1.8 steals & 1 block), but needs to fully commit himself to maximizing his potential on that end. That’s a bit of a microcosm for his total game as Stokes has more sheer talent than anyone in the country and while he’s continued to grow his offensive game with each passing year, he now just needs to prove that he can consistently drive winning.

Crumble is a mismatch scoring forward who averaged 16.6 points and 7.4 rebounds per game in Pro 16 play. He can play with his back to the basket, face-up and attack off the dribble, or make an occasional spot-up three. He’s almost ambidextrous from 8-10 feet and in, both with his short floater game off the dribble and baby hooks with his back to the basket. He loves to post on the right block, where he can turn his right shoulder into the middle of the lane, or spin baseline. He’s also crafty with his footwork, able to navigate the lane with his gathers, and has a clever lay-up package for his size. He’s a capable shooter, but still a bit inconsistent, with a wide-base and short-armed release (29% 3pt on 2.4 attempts per game and 62% FT). He also shows flashes of passing, but can be very turnover prone (1.8 assists vs. 3.5 turnovers). Crumble is adequate athletically, but far from a standout, and also older for his grade. He’s fully mature physically and yet could still sculpt his frame. While he has offensive versatility, he’s best suited defending opposing fours, and even at that has plenty of room to keep growing on that end. He is, however, a very good rebounder on both ends of the floor.

Thompson is a skilled lefty forward and natural scorer with extreme length. He’s a potent shot-maker who leans back into a high release that is virtually unblockable, allowing him to make threes and pull-ups with very little separation. While his tough shot-making ability is his best offensive asset, we’re gradually starting to see him put more pressure on the rim. He has a terrific right hand in the paint, increasing vertical pop at the rim, and the ability to decelerate and maneuver the lane with euros and long strides. This 3SSB season he averaged 22 points per game on 53% shooting, 39% from three, and 88% from the free-throw line. Physically, he’s grown to 6-foot-8 with shoes on, and has a massive 7-foot-3 wingspan, which allows him to play both the three and the four. That overlap of length and shot-making is rare, especially at this early stage. Thompson is also young for his grade, and won’t turn 18 until August of 2026, so he’s still growing into his body and learning to be physical. Defensively, he has great playmaking metrics (1.9 blocks and 1.6 steals), and is particularly adept at flying out to block shots on the perimeter. His length allows him to close gaps and erase mistakes in a hurry, but too often that’s because he’s not fully locked in for the entire possession. That’s almost a microcosm of his total game as there are moments when his motor and body language can be inconsistent, and yet his overlap of shot-making, scoring efficiency, length, and defensive playmaking is potentially unique in this class.

Branch is a big wing who is long and skilled with developing guard skills. His shooting is a legitimate, and still ascending, weapon. He leans back into his shot more so than he does rise up, but his release is very fluid. He’s coming off a 3SSB season in which he connected on 39% of his threes on 5 attempts per game. He can also create space in the mid-range area with his step-back, albeit without overwhelming lift into his pull-up game. He’s not necessarily a dynamic creator just yet, but he’s smooth with the ball, handles to both sides, and typically able to keep his eyes up. So, while he’s probably best described as a wing right now, he’s gradually developing into more of a big guard. Branch also moves well without the ball at an early stage, is willing to take smaller defenders into the post, and has advanced footwork. Defensively, his size, length, and fluidity give him a ton of potential. Physically, he has terrific measurables at 6-foot-7 (in shoes) with a 7-foot-1 wingspan and good natural frame that is only just beginning to fill out. Between his positional size, great length, shot-making, and developing guard skills, Branch has an undeniably high upside. He’s also very young for his grade, following his reclassification, and won’t turn 18 until late October of his freshman year in college. Thus, he has even more runway than most to keep turning his undeniable potential into consistent production that can drive winning.

Moesch is undersized and not a dynamic athlete, but the southpaw separates himself with his skill and mind for the game. He has elite feel and craft. He’s a maestro making reads out of ball-screens, a creative passer off the dribble, has a ton of finishing tricks with his lay-up package at the rim (58% FG at the rim), and is a much better shooter than he showed in the UAA (27% 3pt on over 5 attempts per game). He’s even got some flair and style to his game when everything is clicking. When Moesch is playing with confidence and rhythm, he’s a threat to make threes in bunches off both the catch and the dribble. That can open up a lot of other aspects of his attack. With deceptive speed and quickness, he doesn’t necessarily struggle to get to the paint, but can get swallowed up at times. His lack of ideal physical gifts can also create some issues on the defensive end of the floor, particularly if he’s alone in space, but he’s plenty competitive and equally smart on that end.

Anderson is a skilled perimeter forward with extreme gravity from behind the arc. He shot 45% from three during the 3SSB season, including a whopping 50% on unguarded catch-and-shoot threes, and 90% at the free-throw line. He also has a feel for the game to really thrive in offensive structure. He’s fluid putting the ball on the floor to attack close-outs, passes well (including while going through the lane), and knows how to move without the ball. He’s more athletic in space than among bodies right now, but he has solid size at 6-foot-7, especially if he can evolve into a true big wing, and a sturdy build. Defensively, he might be best suited at the four right now but gives a diligent effort, contests shots, and generally keeps people in front.

Derkack’s defining characteristics are his hyper-competitiveness and high motor. He has good perimeter size, solid athleticism, and plays in constant attack mode on both ends of the floor. So, while he needs to get stronger and continue to polish his skill-set, he’s already impactful and has significant room for future growth. While not a dynamic vertical athlete, he gets a ton of points in the paint because he slashes well and is an exceptional cutter, with a knack for getting easy buckets by moving off the ball. His ability to improvise and make quick reads on the move is a work in progress, especially when the ball is in his left hand, but it is the shooting which remains his biggest swing skill long-term. He still has a noticeable hitch in his release, particularly when shooting off the catch and even at the free-throw line (21% 3pt on 3.6 attempts per game & 61% FT). While he hasn’t filled out his frame yet and can be bumped off his spots on occasion, he’s not going to back down from anyone. He’s tough with a fiery court demeanor, a nearly 6-foot-10 wingspan, solid defensive playmaking instincts (1.4 steals), and a good nose for the ball as a perimeter rebounder (5.7 per game).

Mgbako is a big-bodied volume scorer on the wing. He has good size at 6-foot-6 with solid length and a naturally stronger frame. His bread and butter offensively has historically come in the mid-range area, where he’s very adept at getting to his spots and then utilizing his size to score over top of contesting defenders with his pull-up. While he’s showing signs of extending his range and generally shoots a soft ball, he can be reluctant to shoot from three or lean back into a snapped release, and converted just 24% of his attempts in EYBL play because of it. Mgbako is also starting to show some potential as a jumbo initiator. He is an excellent wing rebounder who can push the ball up the floor and start the break himself. He’s a better handler and passer than he often gets credit for, knows how to utilize his natural strength, and get his defender on his hip. While he lacks great athletic pop in the lane, he compensates with soft natural hands and touch, along with a good left hand. Defensively, he’s more switchable up than line-up than down, but he’s prioritized his conditioning in recent years, become more mobile sliding on the perimeter because of it, is a willing talker, and has good hands (1.1 steals and 1.7 blocks in EYBL play).

Pendergrass is an athletic and potentially versatile forward. While his frame may not be flashy, he's older for his grade and physically ahead. He has good bounce around the rim as a finisher and is functionally strong. He's a good rebounder, particularly on the offensive end, where he averaged over 4 per game in Pro16 play. He has mismatch potential with an inside-out scoring flashes, but can get inefficient when hunting tough twos and contested shots. Defensively, he has the physical tools to be effective, but has yet to consistently commit to changing the game on that end of the floor.

Barnes is a playmaking guard who plays in constant attack mode, on both ends of the floor. He has a lot of speed, and sheer burst, that he uses to attack the rim, but can also vary his pace with stop-and-go moves to get a piece of the paint when the game slows down. He’s a capable passer off the dribble, and particularly adept at throwing lobs, but can get wild and turnover prone (3.5 assists vs. 3.3 turnovers in 3SSB play and 3.4 assists vs. 3.4 turnovers in grind session play), which is why he’s not yet considered a primary decision-maker. He’s a high-volume, but streaky, shooter who made 31% of his threes on almost 8 attempts per game in 3SSB play and has converted 34% in grind session play this year. There’s untapped potential from long-range though with a high and fluid release that should translate more efficiency with improved shot-selection. Defensively, he’s an aggressive playmaker who utilizes quick hands and feet to force turnovers. He averaged 1.8 steals in 3SSB and is at a whopping 3.4 in grind session play. Of course, he can be overly aggressive and gamble unnecessarily on that end of the floor at times as well. Long-term, if he can blend his burst, playmaking, on/off ball versatility, defensive instincts, and shooting potential, there’s very clear upside in front of him. The key is putting those together with efficiency, learning when to play at what speed, and simultaneously filling out his frame.

Brutus is a big wing with long arms, athleticism, and a high motor. He’s a versatile defender with the size, strength, and mobility to be very switchable at the next level. He excels in the open court where he can get downhill and finish explosively above the rim. He’s also a good wing rebounder. His half-court skill set needs to keep expanding, particularly when the game slows down. He’s a solid handler and passer within the flow of the offense, but not as dynamic with his first-step as you would expect for someone with his athleticism. He’s gradually making strides with his shooting (31% 3pt & 76% FT), but still a work in progress. The bottom line is that he has the motor to maximize his physical gifts, a ton of defensive tools, and plenty of untapped upside as he continues to develop his offensive game.

Howard is a long and athletic southpaw wing who changes the game on both ends of the floor. He attacks the rim in straight lines, getting downhill and absorbing contact with a physical style. He rises up to finish above the rim and is a lob threat. He also has a translatable shooting stroke and has even shown some flashes of being able to make movement shots (45% 3pt, 75% FT), which is very unusual for someone so young (won’t turn 18 until following his high school graduation) and with such long arms (6-foot-11 wingspan). In addition to his length and physicality, Howard also has a naturally strong frame that is only going to continue to fill-out. Defensively, he’s engaged, excels with his court coverage, and has good playmaking metrics (2 steals and 0.9 steals). There’s still room for him to clean up some of his angles, but he has a world of potential, both on and off the ball. Offensively, he could take his game to new levels by developing his right hand and decision-making, particularly when he’s in attack mode (1.5 assists. Vs. 1.9 turnovers). Overall, Howard was one of the biggest stock-risers of the grassroots season. If he can sustain the recent gains he’s shown, and continue to develop on both ends of the floor, he could be one of the most intriguing long-term prospects in the class.

Holt is physically imposing on the perimeter with a terrific combination of length, strength, athleticism, speed, and a wealth of intangibles. He’s competitive, plays hard, and imposes his will on both ends of the floor. He also has good perimeter size at 6-foot-5-plus with a 6-foot-9 wingspan. He’s a downhill attacker who plays through contact, has decisive burst on his way to the rim, and is an efficient finisher with both hands (68% at the rim in 3SSB play). He came up the ranks as a natural wing, but is working to evolve into more of a true guard. His ball-screen reads have started to improve (2.8 assists vs. 1.8 turnovers) as too has his handle, although still less comfortable in the open court with his left. His shooting may be his biggest swing skill. He has a wide-base and notable dip in his left shoulder, but there’s nonetheless been definite growth. The biggest issue is that he hunts them a little too much, presumably in an attempt to show his improvements, instead of getting downhill at every opportunity (29% 3pt and 82% FT). Defensively, he can be a true standout, utilizing his physical tools to make plays, guard multiple positions, and then rising up for rebounds (7.4 per game). He has great anticipation in passing lanes (2.5 steals) and has an advanced understanding of how to be physical on the ball while simultaneously showing his hands. The bottom line is that Holt is one of the most consistently impactful players in high school basketball and has the mentality and physical tools to have a high long-term floor. His ceiling will be dictated by the continued progression of his guard skills, along with his ability to maintain his efficiency while stretching his game.

The southpaw guard is one of the most prolific scorers in high school basketball. While he may not have overwhelming size or explosiveness, he’s highly skilled and equally crafty with the basketball. He’s a deep shooter, with range well beyond the three-point line, but he also has an exceptionally deep bag to rely on when creating his individual offense. His shot-making off the dribble is extremely potent. He has a shifty handle, can stop quick for tough pull-ups, or create space with step-backs. He has an advanced understanding of how to navigate a ball-screen and a wide assortment of clever tricks including polished footwork and a variety of different fakes. He can also go to his float game or lay-up package when he gets deep enough. Crowe is a volume scorer, who can rattle off buckets in bunches when he gets going, but also has stretches where he can monopolize the ball and hunt shots. There have been times, especially with USA Basketball, where he’s shown floor-vision and passing ability, and that will be an area of his game he’ll likely have to lean more into more in the future. Physically, he needs to continue to get stronger, so that he can absorb contact in the lane and hold up on the defensive end. He’s young for his grade, and already shown some physical maturation, but will need to keep progressing and simultaneously embrace the defensive end of the floor. Overall though, he has a special ability to score the basketball that has produced a record-setting high school career and should translate up levels.

Gaskins is a long, strong, and physical four-man who defends, rebounds, and is continuing to develop his offensive game. His most reliable individual offense is in the mid-range area right now, where he has a high release to score over contesting defenders with pull-ups and in the mid-post. He’s gradually becoming more comfortable extending his range to three, but still needs to get more consistent (28% 3pt & 75% FT in EYBL play). He can put the ball on the floor comfortably with either hand, but isn’t a natural creator or offensive alpha at this point. He also has the potential to be a much better decision-maker than the numbers may indicate. He averaged 1.7 assists against 3 turnovers, but there were still flashes of passing potential and quick processing as a decision-maker when he wasn’t trying to force his individual offense. Defensively, he’s engaged and already versatile. While not necessarily a dynamic athlete or super twitchy mover, he does have good feet when pulled to the perimeter. He keeps people in front of him and does a pretty good job getting around screens for a player his size. He’s even more natural guarding bigger players in the post, where he can be physical and hold his ground without fouling. Away from the ball, he can scramble, cover the court well in long-rotations, and then finish possessions by rebounding in traffic (7.8 per game). Physically, he’s had a naturally stronger build since he was younger and yet still has plenty of room to add muscle mass once he gets into a college weight room. He’s also continued to grow and most recently measured at nearly 6-foot-9 with shoes with a 7-foot-1 wingspan. Finally, he’s young for his grade, and won’t turn 18 until August after his high school graduation, so there is a significant runway for continued development.

Hodge is a power wing who is big and strong with broad shoulders, big hands, and a physically imposing style on both ends of the floor. He can push the ball up the floor himself, gets downhill with a lot of sheer force, and bully smaller defenders, but still has plenty of nuance to his attack. He can navigate traffic in a crowded lane, has good use of both hands, is an efficient finisher (68% at the rim), plays with pretty good feel, and is a capable passer off the dribble. He’s also a very impactful defender who is switchable, particularly up the line-up. He lacks twitchy quickness, but has very good hands, both in passing lanes and on the ball (2.4 steals per game in UAA play), and is an exceptional perimeter rebounder (6.9 per game including 2.1 on the offensive end). The most glaring limitation in Hodge’s game right now is his shooting. He was just 2-22 from behind the arc in UAA play and 33% from the free-throw line. In FIBA play, he was 14-21 from the free-throw line, but still just 3-18 from three. The progression of his free-throw shooting is particularly important given the amount of contact he draws with his attacking style. Hodge is older for his grade (having turned 18 in April of his junior year) and fully developed physically, but his college ready body, size, and strength, gives him positional versatility on both ends of the floor. He also has a wealth of competitive intangibles as a good teammate with toughness and a motor.

-Modern day giant that looks like an offensive tackle playing tight end at 6-foot-7, 250 pounds. -Special athlete for his size that has the hands, coordination and burst to be a matchup nightmare for defenders, especially down in the red zone. -Punishes assignments as an attached blocker as he sets the tone on the corner with a sturdy base and easy downhill force. -Improved route running dexterity between junior and senior seasons, showcasing the ability to be far more than just an underneath option as he got vertical and challenged the seam. -Owns a monster web and needs little space to make acrobatic grabs with his concentration and jump timing. -Not going to win many foot races, but can shed tacklers with his muscle and find extra yardage when targeted in the flats. -Profile aligns with the Y-TEs playing on Sundays these days as he pairs backboard-shattering athleticism with desired aggression. -A move to defense isn’t out of the question given what he has put on tape as a pass rusher. -Projects as an impact player for a College Football Playoff regular that can make a difference in both the pass and run game. Should push for snaps early. More Evaluations Evaluated 02/17/2025 Andrew Ivins Director of Scouting Read Evaluated 06/16/2023 Andrew Ivins Director of Scouting Read

Lanier has a good blend of perimeter size, length, open floor athleticism, a sturdy frame, and some budding guard skills. He shoots a soft ball, with a wider base and consequently less lift into his release. He’s particularly efficient off the catch (42% on catch-and-shoot threes, including 46% when unguarded) and at the free-throw line (84%). He is good enough with the ball to be a secondary handler and can make tough pull-ups off the dribble, but has a tendency to settle for them. He can get loose for some explosive finishes in the open floor, but doesn’t separate as easily in the half-court and can struggle to finish in traffic at times (49% on lay-ups). He has good footwork at various spots on the floor and comes off pin-downs with good speed. Defensively, his tools give him a high ceiling. In addition to his measurables and increasing strength, he moves well laterally and can cover the court. He has active hands, can get into passing lanes (1.5 steals), guard multiple positions, and be switchable. He’s also a good perimeter rebounder (4.1 per game). The bottom line is that he’s trending towards a big guard with a versatile two-way floor game, but is a bit older for his grade, having turned 18 in March of his junior year.

Sterling is a big-time shooter off the basketball who made 44% of his threes, on over 8 attempts per game, during the Pro16 season. He has extreme gravity as a floor spacer (12-18 on open catch-and-shoot threes), but is also a tough shot-maker off the bounce. He’s the type of threat who coaches can run off various types of screening action. Simultaneously, he’s a good ball-handler, who is very adept at probing the lane and holding his dribble until he gets just enough separation for his pull-up. Physically, Sterling has solid positional size, but some athletic limitations, particularly at the rim. He actually shot a lower percentage inside the arc than he did beyond it during Pro16 play. Defensively, there are some correlating concerns as well, especially when he gets extended away from the basket or put in long close-out situations. For whatever physical gifts he may lack, he compensates not just with skill, but intangibles and feel for the game. He passes and cuts well, all while understanding what his niche is, and has been extremely well coached. He’s also an elite competitor with innate competitiveness.

There aren’t many players, or certainly guards, in the country who can match the sheer tools of Austin Goosby. He has very good backcourt size at 6-foot-5, long arms with a 6-foot-9 wingspan, and big hands. He’s a bouncy vertical athlete, a lay-up maker with his dominant right hand, has soft natural hands, and touch. His best attribute though may be his feel for the game. He cuts well, passes well, and instinctively knows how to play in ways that allow him to make quick and efficient decisions with the basketball. While he’s established himself as one of the best perimeter players in the country, there’s still plenty of room for development. While he has soft natural touch, he could be a more consistent shooter. He made just 25% of his 3pt attempts in EYBL play and has a bit of an elongated release, particularly off the dribble, as he tends to both twist and fade. He also tends to be a bit upright off the dribble and fairly right hand dominant, particularly as a finisher. Defensively, he has terrific playmaking metrics (1.6 steals & 1.3 blocks), but a high center of gravity and narrow base in his lower body that leads to some questions about how he’ll handle contact at the next level. He's also going to need to be lower to the ground and more disciplined in his stance. While Goosby will undoubtedly have to get stronger, his ability to play both on and off the ball, quickly read the game, and keep the ball moving once the dominoes have started falling should allow him to make an immediate contribution right away as a freshman. In short, he has a very high floor. If he gets more dynamic with his self-creation or consistent with his shot-making, then there could be star type outcomes that become possible as well.

Landrew is a big-bodied wing with developing guard skills. Measuring in at 6-foot-6 with a 6-foot-10 wingspan, he has a powerful frame from head-to-toe rooted in his years spent on the football field. He brings that same physicality and toughness to the hardwood, but now that he’s focusing exclusively on basketball, his game is starting to blossom at a rapid rate. He’s an ascending shooter with very soft natural touch. He made just under 34% of his attempts from three in 3SSB play, but knocked down 46% of his open catch-and-shoot threes, giving him extreme gravity as a floor-spacer. He still twists into a lower release, which may be why he’s not yet as consistent on the move and especially off the dribble (1-15 on dribble jumpers in 3SSB play). There’s clear confidence though and if he becomes a truly knockdown type shot-maker, it unlocks a lot of higher upside outcomes. He's already a great blend player with a high floor because he’s smart, multi-positional, physical, can grab and go, and offer some secondary creation. He also has a very good left hand, is a terrific offensive rebounder (2.4 per game), starting to flash some off-ball cutting, and an efficient rim finisher (65%), despite not being a dynamic vertical athlete. Defensively, he has good hands (1.3 steals), great strength and balance in his lower body, but lacks ideal foot speed, so more switchable up the line-up than down.

Sanders is a versatile lefty and tough match-up problem. He’s a good athlete and leaper with the size and physical tools to play multiple positions. He can finish explosively above the rim, is a very active offensive rebounder (2.2 per game in Pro16 play), and also provides some rim protection – both on the ball and coming from the weakside of the floor. He can take less mobile defenders off the dribble, but needs to get more consistent finishing through contact at the rim (just 45% on lay-ups). He’s a willing, but unreliable, shooter (24% 3pt on 2.5 attempts per game and 36% FT) and needs to be more efficient with his total offense (38% FG and 1 assist vs. 2.2 turnovers). The bottom line is that he has versatile tools, but is older for his grade (turning 19 in January of his senior year) and needs to be reliable with his productivity.

Kiss may be undersized with a wider frame, but he has a world of game. He’s strong, physical, has sneaky bounce, and terrific hands, but what truly differentiates him are his instincts and feel for the game. His processing ability is off the charts and it’s not until you consistently watch him make high level plays and reads look routine that you start to get a full appreciation for it. Kiss is also a unique match-up because he can be equally impactful at various spots on the floor. He’s a terrific screener and has a knack for finding space to finish inside with precise timing. He’s got a lot of sneaky handles and burst off the bounce, with an unconventional crossover move that comes almost mid-first-step. He’s exceptionally poised in the mid-post with footwork, lower-body strength, and dexterity. He’s also capable of high-level passing reads and crafty improvisation from all over the floor. Now, there’s still plenty to clean up. While he has soft touch, he’s not a consistent three-point shooter yet. He can be turnover prone and struggle to score over length. He could sculpt his frame. He can be foul prone defensively, and while he may be best as a smallball five, he's not a prototypical shot-blocker.
